document.title: Digital Radiography with a Large-Area, Amorphous-Silicon, Flat-Panel X-Ray Detector System

sourceSummary.oneLine: Preliminary report evaluating image quality of a digital radiography system with an amorphous-silicon large-area flat-panel detector.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Clinical literature supporting validation and verification discussion for digital flat-panel X-ray detector technology.
keyPoints: The study investigated image quality of a digital radiography system with an amorphous-silicon, large-area, digital flat-panel detector.
The detector had a 143 um pixel size and an active area of 43 x 43 cm2.
DQE was reported as 70% at near-zero spatial frequency under the selected measurement conditions.
The authors concluded that the technology showed potential for equal or superior image quality compared with conventional screen-film systems and for reduced patient radiation dose.
Integrated digital systems based on flat-panel detector technology were expected to ease radiology department workflow.

concepts.name: Digital radiography
concepts.evidence: KEY WORDS. Digital radiography; flat-panel detector; amorphous silicon; detective quantum efficiency.

concepts.name: Amorphous-silicon flat-panel detector
concepts.evidence: The basic principle of the flat-panel x-ray detector is depicted in Figure 1. It consists of a matrix of photodiodes, made from amorphous silicon, which is covered with a cesium iodide (CsI) scintillator.

concepts.name: Cesium iodide scintillator
concepts.evidence: The scintillator consisted of a layer of structured cesium iodide.

concepts.name: Detective quantum efficiency
concepts.evidence: The spatial frequency–dependent detective quantum efficiency (DQE) is now widely accepted as the single most important physical parameter to characterize the image quality of a medical x-ray system.

concepts.name: Modulation transfer function
concepts.evidence: The spatial resolution capability of a detection system is measured in terms of the modulation transfer function (MTF).

concepts.name: Dynamic range
concepts.evidence: The detector response function (Fig. 4) demonstrates a large dynamic range that is substantially wider than that available for a given screen-film system.

concepts.name: Radiation dose reduction
concepts.pageTarget: Radiation dose reduction in digital radiography
concepts.evidence: Flat-panel detector technology based on CsI and amorphous-silicon photodiodes opens the ground for a reduction in patient dose due to better DQE and fewer repeats.

evidence.claim: The article is a preliminary report published in Investigative Radiology, Volume 35, issue 4, April 2000, pages 260-266.
evidence.evidenceText: [Preliminary Report] Investigative Radiology 발행 호: Volume 35(4), April 2000, pp 260-266
evidence.pageTarget: Digital Radiography with a Large-Area, Amorphous-Silicon, Flat-Panel X-Ray Detector System

evidence.claim: The study objective was to investigate image quality of a digital radiography system with an amorphous-silicon large-area digital flat-panel detector.
evidence.evidenceText: RATIONALE AND OBJECTIVES. To investigate the image quality of a digital radiography system with an amorphous-silicon, large-area, digital flat-panel detector.

evidence.claim: The detector used a matrix of amorphous silicon and a structured cesium iodide scintillator.
evidence.evidenceText: A flat-panel detector based on a matrix of amorphous silicon was integrated into a projection radiography system. The scintillator consisted of a layer of structured cesium iodide.

evidence.claim: The detector had a pixel size of 143 um and an active image area of 43 x 43 cm2.
evidence.evidenceText: The detector used in this investigation had a pixel size of 143 μm and a matrix of 30002 pixels, 5 providing an active area of 43 × 43 cm2.

evidence.claim: The DQE measurement yielded 70% at near-zero spatial frequency under the selected beam quality and dose conditions.
evidence.evidenceText: The DQE adopts values of 70% at near-zero spatial frequency for the chosen beam quality (90-kV tube voltage, 0.6-mm copper prefiltration, and 2.5-μGy system dose)

evidence.claim: The authors reported the flat-panel detector DQE as about twice that of screen-film and storage phosphor systems across the frequency range.
evidence.evidenceText: The high DQE, which is about a factor of two larger than the DQE of screen-film and storage phosphor systems over the whole frequency range

evidence.claim: The flat-panel detector MTF was reported as higher than regular screen-film systems up to the Nyquist limit.
evidence.evidenceText: the detector MTF is equivalent to the MTF of medium screen-film systems up to about 2 lp/mm and is higher than the MTF of regular screen-film systems over the whole frequency range up to the Nyquist limit

evidence.claim: The detector response was highly linear up to about 60 uGy before saturation.
evidence.evidenceText: The detector response is highly linear up to about 60 μGy, above which it turns into saturation.

evidence.claim: Clinical images were acquired at exposure conditions equivalent to 400 speed and radiologists rated image quality equal or superior to conventional images.
evidence.evidenceText: All images were acquired at exposure conditions equivalent to 400 speed. The diagnostic image quality provided by the digital system was highly appreciated by the examining radiologists and was rated equal or superior to conventional images

evidence.claim: The authors concluded that CsI and amorphous-silicon photodiode flat-panel detector technology may reduce patient dose through better DQE and fewer repeats.
evidence.evidenceText: Flat-panel detector technology based on CsI and amorphous-silicon photodiodes opens the ground for a reduction in patient dose due to better DQE and fewer repeats.
evidence.pageTarget: Radiation dose reduction in digital radiography

evidence.claim: The prototype detector was integrated into a THORAX PRO wall stand and a MULTIX PRO P Bucky table.
evidence.evidenceText: The detector was integrated into existing x-ray systems consisting of a wall stand unit (THORAX PRO, Siemens; Erlangen, Germany) and a Bucky table (MULTIX PRO P, Siemens)

reviewFlags.reason: No regulatory authority or jurisdiction is identified in the source text; the document is journal literature rather than an authority-issued regulatory document.
No revision or version is present in the source text.
No standards are cited as applicable standards in the document metadata or article text.

Product model identification is indirect; listed models are system/workstation devices used in the study, while the flat-panel detector itself is described as a prototype developed by Trixell without a model name.
Text contains extraction/OCR artifacts, including Korean Ovid interface text and malformed notation such as '30002 pixels'.
